justification: Chapter 3 is organized around the central thesis that 'Son of God' and other divine titles given to Jesus were pre-existing political designations of Greek and Egyptian kingship, not theological inventions. The chapter has an explicit paragraph 'Jesus is the Son of God' (lines 465-474) arguing that pharaohs were literally called 'Son of Ra' as their title, and that Alexander and the Ptolemies carried the same designation. The extensive Pater Noster section (lines 146-250) and the halo section (lines 272-286) develop the Egyptian solar-royal theology angle in depth. This finding — that Egyptian divine kingship theology (king as simultaneously human and divine) provides institutional precedent for 'Son of God' as political rather than metaphysical — directly strengthens an argument already present in chapter 3 with new evidence from the Egyptian institutional model of divine kingship. Chapter 1 does mention mythological parallels (Apollonius, Dionysus) in the 'Mythical figure' section, but only as part of a survey of scholarly portraits, not as a positive argument for Greek/Egyptian institutional precedent for the title 'Son of God'. The finding's evidence most directly serves chapter 3's constructive thesis.

Chapter 1's 'Mythical figure' section (lines 97-160) already argues that Gospel authors engaged in deliberate literary mimesis of Greek models: 'These parallels follow the structure of deliberate literary mimesis — Mark mapped to the Homeric epics, John to Euripides' Bacchae — not random coincidence' (line 111-112). Lines 107-110 specifically note that the passion narrative echoes the trial of Socrates: 'The nobly suffering teacher brought before the state echoes the trial of Socrates. Philosophers condemned for corrupting youth or challenging gods form a clear cultural backdrop.' This finding — that Luke deliberately reshaped Mark's passion narrative to produce a Socratic/Greek heroic death legible to Greek audiences familiar with Achilles and Socrates — is direct textual evidence supporting the mimesis argument chapter 1 already makes. It fits as new evidence for the existing argument that Gospel authors adapted narratives for Greek audiences through deliberate literary imitation. Chapter 3 does not discuss Lukan redaction of the passion narrative or Socratic framing at all; its focus is on the royal-titular vocabulary applied to Jesus, not on narrative shaping techniques within the Gospel texts.
